stories.title: Tunnel Sleeves
stories.content: I try on the hoodie. It drapes over me like a snuggie and huggies my legs. I stretch my arms until the tips of my fingers poke out of its tunnel sized sleeves. It’s a bit big. I put it back and scan the other pieces in the aisle. Everything is one size fits all, except for me. Typical thrifting.

stories.title: Bookworm
stories.content: I open the book, fanning its pages between my fingers. Something flings out and lands in the spine of page 58. As it inches out, I move closer to get a better look. It’s a bookworm and apparently it's read this one before. I ask it to tell me its favorite parts and it ends up reciting the entire story from beginning, to page 58, to end.

stories.title: Friday Plans
stories.content: It’s Friday! My monstera’s leaves point to the ceiling in excitement. I power my laptop down, push back my chair, and raise my arms to join it in its celebration. I fill its silent hoorays with a loud woohoo. My monstera has the same plans every weekend: drink water, photosynthesize, and sleep. Tonight, I want to watch a recently released movie. So, I turn its pot towards the tv, and together we do something new.

stories.title: Cherry Blossoms
stories.content:
I make my way up the cobblestone path. Ivy lines the buildings on either side. Academics trudge past me, carrying the previous night's debates under their eyes. Their bags are heavy with passion.

The exchange of ideas is palpable. I take a big inhale and smell the season change faintly in the air. It whispers its coming, and I ask when it will make a full appearance. I turn the corner and thousands of freshly bloomed cherry blossoms meet my gaze. It has.

stories.title: Blueberry Lanky
stories.content: It’s Sunday morning, and I’ve left the house early as an eager customer. I stand patiently in line, shifting my feet to keep my balance. If I fell, I wonder if we’d all tumble over like dominos. My eyes feel weary. Still waking up, I lazily scan the menu. There it is. NEW The bold, bright lettering jolts me awake, and my eyes dart past the hyphen that follows it to confirm the menu item. NEW - A blueberry lanky "What’s a Lanky", I say curiously. No one overhears, or if they do, they don’t answer. It’s probably early for them, too. Each month’s special has been unfamiliar, but never unrecognizable. Minutes pass, and I’ve finally reached the front of the line. The store clerk asks me what I want and I timidly respond, "the lanky." My tone arches as I speak, resembling a plea to confirm I haven’t misread the menu. They smile back and say, "Nice choice. That’s my new favorite." As I wait to pay, I feel excitement rush through me from the possibility of having a new favorite too.

stories.title: Slowdance
stories.content: I pull the record from its resting place. It’s an onion I have to peel the layers from: the inner sleeve, the outer sleeve, the protective cover. Finally, I release the vinyl from the walls built around it. I rest my palms on its sides, my arms in front of me like I’m readying myself for a slow dance. I look closely at the worn grooves that line its surface. I hope it’ll play. I put it on the turn table and watch it spin. As it speeds up, the light overhead begins to catch its edges and sparkle. Slowly, I lower the needle. Faint crackles transform into a symphony of sound. It’s time for that slow dance.

stories.title: Stick in a Bag
stories.content:
She pulls the chair out at the table in front of me and hangs her bag on its back. She sits and scoots forward. The smell of pinewood greets my nose, and I feel my body relax. The room has transformed into a dense grove of trees. The walls, now a dense brush, seem to separate the rest of the world from me and her. It’s just us and the trees.

I look towards the woman and watch her fumble through her bag. She lifts her hand out, gripping a small stick. Placing it on the table, she tries to catch the pine needles that sprinkle down like fresh snow. Mesmerized, I decide to approach her. I stand up and my feet press into the carpet. I’m walking on moss.

"That’s a lovely stick", I say. I must sound ridiculous.

"Thank you," she says blushing, "It’s quite silly right?" Maybe she thinks she sounds ridiculous, too. "Part of me lives with nature. When I come back to the city, I leave a piece of myself behind and take a piece of it with me so we can stay connected."

"Not silly at all." I pause. “Everyone needs connection”, I say. My mind wanders briefly to the things in the world I’d give a part of myself to so we’d remain linked. I’m interrupted before I come up with an answer.

"Wanna take a seat? Coffee is always better shared in company," she says.

Maybe her.

stories.title: My Saddle
stories.content: Whenever I ride my bike, I feel the world pass by more quickly. My wheels spin like tiny planets, and, with each orbit, they propel me closer to my destination. Friends that lived too far now feel like neighbors. Restaurants that were tucked away now envelop me with mouthwatering scents. Everything seems to come together whenever I get on my saddle. Feeling the sun on my back and the wind in my face, I can’t help but feel a part of it all.

stories.title: Another You
stories.content: Mary had never left Grand Heights before. As she crossed the Crown Bridge to the world beyond, her mind drew pictures of what she might encounter. She was sure there’d be the bad, the good, and most definitely the mundane. But she was most curious if there’d be another you.

stories.title: Grand Heights
stories.content: Mary had never left Grand Heights before. As she crossed the Crown Bridge to the world beyond, her mind drew pictures of what she might encounter. She was sure there’d be the bad, the good, and most definitely the mundane. But she was most curious if there’d be another you.

stories.title: The Mysterious Melody
stories.content:
It must have started at night, because my dreams became lined with unfamiliar sounds. They waltzed through the scenes I constructed in my imagination. And when I awoke, all I could remember were the footsteps they’d left behind.

I spent my waking hours trying to remember the melody, humming random notes in the hopes of forming it accidentally. And it wasn’t until I slept again that I'd remember. This went on for weeks, until one night, I awoke in a shiver. I lay uncovered on the bed. The comforter was a bunched up mound opposite to me. It rose and fell rhythmically. I tugged at it, but it wouldn’t budge. I yanked harder, and then I heard it - a series of delicate notes that stretched like knitwear being pulled apart before receding back to their snug position. I sank into my mattress, letting the melody wash over me and cover me like a blanket. That night I fell asleep to my girlfriend snoring, and when I awoke, I remembered her song completely.

stories.title: Crumble
stories.content: Today I crumbled, but not like a cookie. Maybe more like a scone, or a stale muffin that leaves your mouth dry. It happened all at once. I broke into pieces so small, light wind would have carried me away. Luckily, you witnessed my dusting. You grabbed the broom and swept me up. You cupped me in your hands and held me together until I could do the same. Thank you.

stories.title: Rainbow Hole
stories.content: If I was a black hole, I think I’d change my color, but I’m not sure which. If I was blue, I’d look like an ocean that suddenly drops off at its edges. Anything that tried to float on my surface would be pulled into a deep abyss. It’d be too dramatic. If I was yellow, I could pretend to be a star. I’d probably be famous on some planets. Maybe I could even say I’m a distant relative of the sun. The telescopes that would gawk at me would be so annoying, though. If I was red, I’d look like a scab that the universe picked off before letting it fully heal. Gross. All I really want is to be unique against the black backdrop behind me. I want to be more than nothing, just enough to be something. Maybe I’d be a rainbow hole.

stories.title: Sidwalk Decor
stories.content: My foot was inches away from crushing it. Fortunately, when I walk the neighborhood, I carefully scan the sidewalk for hazards. Sidewalks require a certain level of vigilance. Glass, poop (not just dog), and half eaten sandwiches are regular street decor I dodge, but pretty much anything you wouldn’t want to step on could be something you’d step on. Still, I was surprised when a snail became part of that category. I caught a glimpse of its shiny shell just as I was preparing to plant my foot. I couldn't tell if it was frozen in shock or if it moved that slowly. My foot cast a shadow above it, moving lower. Then, I saw the future headlines in the paper: "Sidewalk snail murderer comes out of his shell." I quickly pulled back my foot and bent down to meet it face on. It stared at me, mouth agape, like it was put off that I’d even tried to walk in its path. Embarrassed, I stood back up and continued walking, hoping that we’d never cross paths again.

stories.title: Fire Feeding
stories.content: It started with a lone spark. It wasn’t long until there was a crowd. A racket of flames conversed. They bounced fervorously as I continued to feed them logs. Their crackles grew, expressing their satiated hunger and delight in the taste of freshly cut wood. It was a 5 course meal, and after I’d depleted the bundles of wood, I threw in a few chocolate wrappers as dessert. I sat by the fire and watched it lull itself to sleep, until it was nothing more than embers.

stories.title: A Hanging Sky
stories.content:
My toes wiggled into the warm sand, burrowing for a light rest. I sat down and laid back, crossing my arms behind my head. The sky above looked closer than ever. I raised my hand, skimming its surface. Droplets of water latched onto my fingers as they cut through the buttery clouds. It all looked so delicate, like a spider hanging from a single thread. At any moment it could fall.

After bathing in the sun for a few hours, I was ready to pack up and leave. But, I was worried I might bump my head. Sucking all the air into my lungs that I could, my cheeks and chest ballooned. In one exhale. I pushed all the air upwards, and with it, the sky. I could no longer reach out and touch it, but even at a distance, its turquoise blue was enchanting. I spent the rest of the afternoon laying there in awe.

stories.title: The Coatrack
stories.content:
Tucked away in the corner of the entry way, it sat nearly empty.

In the dead of winter, when the coats resurged from the closet, it would bear a heavier load. Parties, though they rarely occurred, brought in couture coats it blushed holding. Blizzards came with thick and flannel lined jackets that made it stronger. Each time the front door opened, it'd eagerly gaze at the layers being worn and flex its branches to support them. But it only felt warmth from the household’s residents in the coldest of times. When the flowers bloomed, it'd lose its coats, like a dog shedding its fur. And once again, it’d go unnoticed.

In its off season, its expectations hung low. Until, one sunny afternoon, a tote bag was put on it. The next day, a bike helmet. A new visitor was frequenting the household. Quickly, the new visitor became an acquaintance, a regular, and then a fixture of the household. With each day, as they came and went, something new was added to the coatrack's branches. A hula-hoop, a scarf, a phone charger, a keychain, and for a short time, a candy necklace.

It grew bigger week over week and became a visual centerpiece of the entryway. At the end of each day, for the first time, it felt full. Now, when the household awoke, the rack would stretch its arms excitedly, making room for the day’s addition, whatever it may be.

stories.title: Biggest Thumb
stories.content:
His thumb looked bigger. It was a little too long. He put his hands together to compare. It was longer; he was sure of it.

The next day, Peter measured again. His thumb nearly reached the knuckle of his index finger now. He immediately scheduled an appointment with his doctor.

"I’ve seen this before," his doctor said, while pulling and prodding at his thumb.

 "Your thumb wants to be more like your other fingers. You should be glad your other fingers don’t want to be more like your thumb. The stubby route would be quite the disaster."

How was this not a disaster? Peter panicked. This was a catastrophe. 12 unhelpful minutes later and $350 poorer, Peter was on his way home no closer to solving his thumb’s sudden reach for new heights. With no other ideas to stop or stunt it, he let it grow.

Soon enough, Peter found advantages to his thumb’s new length. His grip strength was superb, he was unbeatable at thumb wars, and his pointing never went unnoticed. Eventually, Peter began to wonder when his other thumb would catch up in height. Of all his fingers, especially his biggest thumb, it felt a little too short.

stories.title: Bed Dough
stories.content:
Some nights, I wish I slept on bread dough. It’d be my loaf of bed. Its thick, round edges would rise from the floor each morning as the sun peeked through the window. Slowly, it’d grow until it met my night stand.

When dusk came, it’d be light and fluffy. With a slight dome at its center, I’d be ready to sink into it. After brushing my teeth and putting on pajamas, I’d take a running start and jump into bed. My body would spring back from its surface and then gently lower. The bed would compress like a pressed bubblewrap that won’t pop. I’d fall asleep instantly.

I’d wake in the morning and listen to the low hums and gurgles of the dough rising again. They’d be an alarm I’d never have to set. When I’d gain the strength to rise out of bed, I’d sit up and feel the dough stick to my back, trying to pull me back into a doze. And some days maybe I’d give in and spend all day in my load of bed.

stories.title: Girly Things
stories.content: They were out and about doing girly things. None of them had ever discussed what a girly thing was, but when one of them said it, they all nodded in unison. Tonight, they found themselves at Riley’s. It was a small bar in a small town. But for this group it was bigger. It was the place for girly things.

stories.title: Her Voice
stories.content: My grandma used to compliment me on my memory of people. She was forgetful, but even in her last days, she remembered the sound of my voice. She had always prided herself on recognizing voices. She told me that even when I was a baby, talking gibberish and giggling uncontrollably, my chords were unique against the backdrop of the household and streetside sounds. Sometimes, I wonder if we would’ve made good detectives, partnering to identify the world’s loudest most distinguished-looking criminals. I can recognize her face anytime, anywhere. When I close my eyes I see her radiant smile. Sometimes, I hear a voice telling me I’m loved, and I know she’d recognize it as her own, so I do too.

stories.title: My Shadow
stories.content:
My shadow is gone. It disappeared after a scuttle we had last week. I thought it may have been hiding in my dresser or blending in with other shadows in the house, but I’ve exhausted all the corners and crevices in my apartment that it could be in. We’ve had arguments before, but never like this.

Some nights, when I lay awake covered in darkness, I feel it’s there. But, when I turn on the lights, I see nothing. I miss my shadow. It always followed my lead and kept me company. I hope that one day, when I look over my shoulder, I’ll see it there once more.
